project: Raising the Cost of Offline Password Cracking

experiment_context.purpose: Controlled demonstration of offline cracking cost using synthetic users and passwords.
experiment_context.ethical_boundary: No real systems, accounts, or credentials are attacked.

storage_results.label: Plain text
storage_results.summary: Database leak directly exposes the original password.
storage_results.security_role: Failure case used as a comparison reference.

storage_results.label: Salted SHA-256
storage_results.summary: A fast general-purpose hash. Salt helps, but fast verification still helps offline attackers.
storage_results.security_role: Shows why hash alone is not enough for password storage.

storage_results.label: bcrypt
storage_results.summary: Adaptive password hashing with a cost factor.
storage_results.security_role: Raises offline guessing cost after a database leak.

storage_results.label: Argon2id
storage_results.security_role: Raises CPU and memory cost for offline guessing.

policy_results.label: Complexity rule
policy_results.description: At least 8 characters with uppercase, lowercase, digit, and symbol.

policy_results.label: Length-first rule
policy_results.description: At least 14 characters. Allows long password phrases.

policy_results.label: Blocklist rule
policy_results.description: At least 8 characters and not common, breached, or context-specific.

policy_results.label: Layered policy
policy_results.description: At least 12 characters, blocklisted passwords rejected, long password phrases allowed.

system_assessment.password_policy: Complexity rule: uppercase, lowercase, number, symbol

attack_chain_summary.main_finding: The stronger control is raising offline cracking cost after a database leak.
risk_reduction_story: Blocklists and long-password-friendly rules improve password choice before storage.
bcrypt or Argon2id increases attacker cost during offline cracking.
Slow password hashing reduces the number of cracked accounts under the same budget.
The report records later login-stage controls as out-of-scope analysis rather than dashboard evidence.
